config.title: Kurfürstendamm Berlin: FAQ
config.subtitle: Straight answers on where the Kurfürstendamm starts, how much of it is worth walking, what is free, how to get there, and why West Berlin ended up with a second city centre.

items.a: Yes, but plan a short visit rather than a completion walk. The Memorial Church, Bikini Berlin, the zoo entrance, C/O Berlin and Zoologischer Garten station sit within about 300 metres of Breitscheidplatz. Give that cluster an hour or two, then turn north toward Savignyplatz or take the train.

items.q: How long is the Kurfürstendamm and how long does it take to walk?
items.a: The Kurfürstendamm itself runs 3.5 km from Breitscheidplatz to Rathenauplatz. Together with Tauentzienstraße, the walking axis from Wittenbergplatz to Rathenauplatz is about 4.1 km. The useful visitor walk is Wittenbergplatz to Uhlandstraße, about 1.3 to 1.4 km or roughly 18 minutes.

items.q: Where does the Kurfürstendamm start and end?
items.a: It starts at Breitscheidplatz beside the Kaiser Wilhelm Memorial Church and ends at Rathenauplatz. Wolf Vostell's two concrete Cadillacs stand in the traffic circle there. They are one of three surviving works from the 1987 Skulpturenboulevard project.

No. KaDeWe stands on Tauentzienstraße at Wittenbergplatz. The Kurfürstendamm begins farther west at Breitscheidplatz. Berliners often call the combined axis "Ku'damm" in conversation, but they are two different streets.

items.q: How do I get to the Kurfürstendamm from Alexanderplatz?
items.a: The direct S-Bahn from Alexanderplatz to Zoologischer Garten normally takes about 15 to 20 minutes, and the trip stays inside zone AB. U2 and U9 also serve Zoo. U1 and U9 stop at Kurfürstendamm station, about 200 metres west of Breitscheidplatz.

items.q: What can you do on the Kurfürstendamm for free?
items.a: Entry to the Kaiser Wilhelm Memorial Church is free. The new church is normally open daily from 10:00 to 18:00; the ruined tower's Memorial Hall publishes separate opening days, so check the official notice before going inside. Bikini Berlin's free terrace is listed daily from 9:00 to 21:00 and looks across into the zoo. The direct monkey-enclosure view is from the panoramic Monkey Window inside the lower mall.

items.q: Is the Kurfürstendamm better for shopping than Mitte?
items.a: It depends what you are shopping for. The Kurfürstendamm and Tauentzienstraße still hold the big international chains, the luxury boutiques and KaDeWe, so for department stores and brand names the west is stronger. For independent shops, second-hand and design, the streets around Mitte, Prenzlauer Berg and Kreuzberg are better. Do not travel west purely to browse; go for the church and the cluster around it, and treat the shops as a bonus.

items.q: Why does Berlin have two city centres?
items.a: The Kurfürstendamm was already a major shopping, theatre and cinema street before the war. During the division, much of the historic core around Unter den Linden and Museum Island lay in East Berlin, while the area around Zoo, the Memorial Church, Tauentzienstraße and the Kurfürstendamm became West Berlin's principal commercial and cultural centre. After reunification Mitte regained importance, but City West remained active.

items.q: What is worth seeing at the western end of the Kurfürstendamm?
items.a: The Schaubühne at Lehniner Platz occupies Erich Mendelsohn's curved Universum cinema from 1928; selected performances have English surtitles, so check the programme first. Buses M19 and M29 stop at Lehniner Platz. At Rathenauplatz, the concrete Cadillacs mark the end of the street, with S Halensee about 400 metres away.
